The threats to encryption are already apparent, such as "store-first, decrypt-later" attacks; while digital signatures represent a future threat, requiring a transition to PQC before deployment to a Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC). Therefore, Google has adjusted its threat model, prioritizing the migration of authentication services to PQC—a crucial component of cybersecurity and digital signature migration. II. Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computers (CRQC) Are Approaching Reality A blog post and accompanying white paper published on March 31, 2026, indicate that future quantum computers may require fewer qubits and quantum gates than ever before to break elliptic curve cryptography used to protect cryptocurrencies and other systems. In the white paper, Google shares updated estimates of the quantum computing "resources" (i.e., qubits and quantum gates) required to break the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP-256, the foundation of elliptic curve cryptography). Google uses the number of logical qubits (error-correcting qubits consisting of hundreds of physical qubits) and Tooffoli gates (expensive basic operations performed on qubits, a major driver of execution time for many algorithms) to represent resource estimates. Specifically, two quantum circuits (a series of quantum gates) implementing Shor's algorithm were written to crack ECDLP-256: one circuit uses fewer than 1200 logical qubits and 90 million Tooffoli gates, and the other uses fewer than 1450 logical qubits and 70 million Tooffoli gates. Google estimates that, under the assumption that hardware performance meets the standards of some of Google's flagship quantum processors, these circuits can execute in minutes using superconducting qubit CRQC with fewer than 500,000 physical qubits. This is approximately 20 times fewer physical qubits than required to solve ECDLP-256. Google stated that with the continuous advancement of science and technology, cryptography-related quantum computers (CRQC) are gradually approaching reality, necessitating a transition to PQC. This is the reason Google recently released its 2029 migration timeline. These two Google blog posts mark the tech giant's formal shift of the quantum threat from a "theoretical vision" to a "close-up engineering task." What does this mean for BTC and ETH? The core signature algorithms of Bitcoin and Ethereum are both ECDSA (based on secp256k1 elliptic curves), and public keys are typically exposed in transactions/addresses (especially in traditional addresses and some Taproot scenarios). Once quantum computers reach cryptographically correlated quantum computing (CRQC), they can reverse-engineer private keys from public keys, enabling the theft of funds or the forgery of transactions. This poses a direct threat to addresses with exposed public keys ("harvest now, decrypt later" risk). The common impacts include: 1. Fund security risk: Cold wallets/unspent UTXOs are relatively secure if the public key is not exposed, but most on-chain assets are exposed. After Q-Day, addresses that have not been upgraded may be stolen by quantum attacks. 2. Trust and adoption impact: Institutional investors may avoid chains that are "quantum insecure," triggering capital outflows or price volatility. 3. "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later": Attackers can collect encrypted data/public keys now and crack them later when quantum computers mature. While both Bitcoin and Ethereum face common threats, their responses differ significantly. This may be rooted in their community cultures. BTC faces higher risks and slower responses. The Bitcoin community is more conservative and highly decentralized; upgrades require network-wide consensus (soft fork/hard fork), and currently lack a coordinated roadmap. Google's move is seen as a wake-up call for Bitcoin developers; many Bitcoin community members still believe 2029 is too aggressive and the quantum threat is exaggerated, but Google's hardware progress has led some to reassess. Current status: No official network-wide PQC migration plan; progress is slow. Proposals such as BIP 360 provide partial quantum protection for Taproot's critical path costs, but a complete upgrade still requires community consensus. Possible paths: Introduce new opcode/address types via a soft fork to support PQC signatures; or develop a quantum-safe wallet/address format. Some developers are already discussing this, but the decentralized nature makes coordination difficult and time-sensitive. Challenges: The Bitcoin community is deeply divided (many believe that quantum computers cannot reach scale in the short term), and if no action is taken before 2029, it may face an "existential threat" and a loss of institutional confidence. ETH is relatively better prepared; a roadmap has been developed. Ethereum has been laying the groundwork for 8 years, and the Ethereum Foundation (EF) recently released its Post-Quantum Ethereum roadmap, which is highly aligned with Google's 2029 timeline. EF believes that quantum "cryptography-related" threats may occur in 8-12 years, but "work must begin now." Roadmap: EF has launched a dedicated website (pq.ethereum.org) and a "Strawmap"/four-phase (or seven hard fork) roadmap, aiming to complete the Layer 1 protocol-level upgrade (including validator signatures, accounts, data storage, and proof system) by 2029. A full migration of the execution layer may take several more years. Specific Measures: Use STARK-based or PQC signatures; implement gradually through multiple hard forks (such as "I", "J", etc.); testnet is already running. Vitalik Buterin and others have publicly discussed quantum protection multiple times. Advantages: Smart contract platform upgrades are more flexible, with a clear timeline and resources, and progress far exceeds that of Bitcoin. Conclusion: Regardless of whether it's BTC or ETH, users should pay attention to quantum-safe wallets (such as hardware wallets that support PQC signatures) as soon as possible and migrate their assets after the protocol upgrade. Google's announcement has accelerated the quantum protection process across the entire crypto industry—Ethereum is already taking action, and Bitcoin needs to follow suit as soon as possible, otherwise the risks will be significantly amplified. In the coming years, PQC compatibility will become a standard feature of blockchains. If quantum hardware continues to advance at the current pace, 2029 will be a significant watershed year in the history of cryptocurrency security.

data.title: Delphi Digital: Stablecoins – The Next Frontier in Financial Infrastructure
data.description:
Author: Delphi Digital, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Stablecoin issuers have now become the 19th largest holders of US Treasury bonds, and their assets are exactly the same as the underlying assets supporting ordinary savings accounts. One of the most important issues in current cryptocurrency legislation is how these institutions are allowed to dispose of their Treasury bond yields.

Yield Gap

There is a huge interest rate differential between US Treasury bond yields and the deposit rates that banks pay to depositors. Treasury bond yields remain in the 3.5%–4% range, while the average yield of ordinary savings accounts is only 0.39%.

Stablecoin issuers hold the same underlying assets. The GENIUS Act prohibits them from distributing yields directly to holders, and regulators are expanding the scope of this ban to include various circumvention methods that have emerged since then.

If people can hold funds that are readily liquidated, have no credit risk, and generate interest, their willingness to keep cash in traditional bank accounts will decrease significantly.

Critics characterize this as a national security issue.

On a practical level, it poses a threat to the fractional-reserve financing model upon which the entire credit system depends. Structural financing problems do exist; banks rely on deposits to issue loans. If a large influx of dollars into fully-reserve stablecoins invested solely in government bonds occurs, private credit creation will contract. Funds will shift to sovereign assets, and the credit transmission mechanism supporting SME lending and consumer credit will begin to fail.

Small and medium-sized banks and credit unions will be the first to be affected. They rely on deposits far more than large institutions to support their lending businesses. Once these deposits flow onto the blockchain, they will not only lose interest rate spreads but also their corresponding lending capacity.

This is the real core contradiction in the stablecoin legislation debate: End users can obtain lower-cost, faster-circulating US dollars, but the credit system will lose the deposit base needed to maintain lending.

This is precisely the core contradiction in the stablecoin legislation debate: End users can obtain lower-cost, faster-circulating US dollars, but the credit system will lose the deposit base needed to maintain lending.

Everyone Wants to Be a Bank. Over the past few months, fintech companies and crypto businesses have been vying for banking licenses by directly applying for licenses or acquiring existing banks. Following the global financial crisis, the US virtually stopped issuing commercial banking licenses for nearly a decade. The number of new banks established plummeted from approximately 132 per year to just 6. When license resources dried up, the market stopped trying to become a bank and instead built systems around banks; now, the situation is completely reversed. For crypto-native companies and stablecoin issuers, a federal license means access to the Federal Reserve's instant payment systems FedNow and Fedwire, and direct control over the clearing and settlement layer. For traditional fintech companies, this means breaking free from dependence on third-party banks and achieving full-chain vertical integration. This trend of convergence is progressing in both directions. In December 2025, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) passed a proposed rule allowing state banks under its supervision to issue payment-type stablecoins through subsidiaries. In February 2026, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) followed suit, introducing similar proposed rules for national banks. Once these rules are finalized, any licensed depository institution will have a legitimate channel to issue stablecoins. The End of Vertical Integration: Stripe is the most typical example. Through acquisitions of Bridge, Privy, and Metronome, and its partnership with Paradigm to create Tempo, it is building a complete payment infrastructure from stablecoin issuance to merchant settlement.

It's not just them that are moving in this direction. The stablecoin technology architecture highly corresponds to the institutional financial system: blockchain settlement replaces the Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) system, stablecoin issuance replaces commercial bank deposits, on-chain liquidity and foreign exchange trading replace agency banking services, compliance logic replaces anti-money laundering (AML) infrastructure, and various payment applications are built on top of all of this.

Major institutions in the fintech, banking, and crypto sectors are all trying to control as many levels of ecosystem discourse power as possible.

**Issuer Risk Becomes a New Type of Credit Risk** Tether and Circle together account for over 84% of the total stablecoin market capitalization. The brief de-pegging of USDC during the Silicon Valley Bank bankruptcy vividly demonstrated this **concentration risk**. Although Circle was solvent and had sufficient reserves, some of its funds were temporarily trapped in troubled banks and could not be accessed. In the traditional banking system, payment risk is distributed among various institutions, while credit risk is distributed across the credit assets of thousands of banks. However, in the stablecoin system, the settlement risk between participants is eliminated, but it is concentrated at the issuer level. The system does not achieve zero risk; rather, the risk has been vertically transferred—from dispersed counterparty exposure to highly concentrated issuer-dependent risk. The issuance of white-label stablecoins is expanding, with institutions such as Paxos, Agora, Brale, M0, and Bridge all offering issuance-as-a-service. Over the past year, Paxos, through partnerships with companies like PayPal, has seen its market capitalization increase nearly eightfold, with partners handling distribution while Paxos focuses on infrastructure and compliance. While this model has proven viable, it hasn't yet shaken the dominance of existing leading institutions. Such a level of liquidity inherently possesses a strong self-reinforcing effect. Conclusion: In just a few years, stablecoins have grown from niche trading tools into a crucial infrastructure for cross-border remittances in emerging markets. The next wave of impact will directly target the core financing model of the banking system itself. The GENIUS Act has established a regulatory framework, and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) is currently developing implementation rules, with a finalization scheduled for July 2026. The remaining key variable is whether the CLARITY Act will pass. If approved, it will directly determine whether various platforms can offer any form of stablecoin yield.

data.title: Why does Wu Jihan prefer manual labor when his peers seek patronage from industry giants?
data.description:
On March 30, 2026, Bitdeer released a stunning set of Nordic-style remodeling blueprints that shocked the market. They announced that their subsidiary, TDC, has commissioned Norwegian contractor DCI to transform an existing facility in Tydal, Norway, into a 180 MW AI data center, expected to be completed in December 2026. The center will primarily run NVIDIA's latest Vera Rubin AI platform. But this isn't a story of money talks. Bitdeer's total debt has already exceeded $1.3 billion. On one hand, there's the ledger nearing its red line; on the other, there's the massive investment in top-tier European computing infrastructure. What's the real motive behind this stark contrast? The root of all these actions lies in the precipitous decline of BitDeer's old business. In April 2024, Bitcoin underwent its fourth halving, reducing the block reward from 6.25 to 3.125. Revenue was instantly halved, but electricity costs and equipment depreciation remained unchanged. In the fourth quarter of 2025, BitDeer's gross profit margin for its mining business dropped to 4.7%. This means that for every 100 yuan earned, less than 5 yuan remained after deducting costs. Even more serious was the fact that other fronts were also starting to succumb to the pandemic. The third-quarter report released on November 10, 2025, showed that the company's self-developed A4 chip was significantly delayed, resulting in a loss per share of US$1.28, far exceeding the expected loss of US$0.22. The stock price fell by 14.9% the following day. Immediately following, on the afternoon of November 11th, a fire broke out at its mine under construction in Ohio, causing two buildings to collapse. To make matters worse, starting in December 2025, multiple law firms filed class-action lawsuits, accusing the company of misappropriating funds from June 6, 2024 to November 10, 2025, regarding A4. Misleading statements regarding the technological maturity and mass production timeline of the chips. Profits are being squeezed to the extreme, and the core business is facing constant turmoil. With cash flow already so tight, why make such a large-scale foray into Norway? II. The most valuable asset isn't the mining machines, but cheap electricity. Because going to Norway is actually about finding a breakthrough. BitDeer announced on March 30, 2026, that it would transform its existing 175 MW mining facility in Tydal, Norway, into a 180 MW AI data center. "AI server rooms are most susceptible to heat and consume the most electricity." An industry insider pointed out, "The industrial electricity price in Norway is approximately $0.04-0.09 per kilowatt-hour, and the average annual temperature is about 5.8°C, essentially acting as a natural air conditioner." With this natural environment, their liquid cooling system can achieve a PUE of 1.1, meaning it costs very little. Of the 1 yuan electricity bill, more than 90 cents were used for real work. Furthermore, the local electricity is almost 100% hydroelectric, and the waste heat from the server room can be transferred to nearby food factories. This kind of "green and environmentally friendly" story is very popular in the capital market. In February 2026, Bitdeer issued $325 million in convertible bonds at an interest rate of 5%. With natural air conditioning and cheap electricity, converting suitable mining farms into AI data centers has become an industry-wide consensus. However, BitDeer has chosen an extremely ambitious path in how to actually do it. By 2026, its competitors were frantically trying to find new solutions. People either become asset-light landlords or seek stability by partnering with tech giants. In June 2024, US-listed mining company Core Scientific signed a 12-year computing power contract worth over $10 billion with a cloud service provider. In December 2025, Canadian mining company Hut 8 signed a $7 billion AI infrastructure agreement with Google as a guarantor. In January 2026, Riot sold 1080 Bitcoins to acquire land in Texas, and then signed a data center lease with AMD with a potential value of $1 billion. However, Wu Jihan, who came from the Bitmain era, still maintained his typical personal style: raising funds, building, and operating the facilities himself. This allows them to fully capitalize on the profits of both upstream and downstream businesses, but it also means shouldering the enormous risks associated with heavy assets all by themselves. As of February 2026, Bitdeer's AI business was running 2096 GPUs with a utilization rate of around 64%, generating approximately $21 million in revenue annually. On the other hand, the over $1.3 billion debt generates $65 million in interest annually. The current AI revenue is only enough to cover about four months of interest. This heavy asset-heavy blueprint has directly divided Wall Street into two sharply contrasting camps.

IV. Nearly 40% Short Selling Rate, All Waiting for a Signature

Some people have seen a severely undervalued goldmine of computing power.

As of March 2026, out of the 12 institutions covering the company, 10 had given it a "Buy" rating with an average target price of $26.6. Investment banks, citing industry benchmarks, point out that the cost of building a new AI data center typically ranges from $8 million to $12 million per megawatt, suggesting that Bitdeer, with its low-cost electricity, has significant room for revaluation. However, another group cast a cold vote of dissent with their hard-earned money. In January 2026, KBW downgraded its rating to $14, and Zacks further issued a "Strong Sell" rating on March 19. The most direct data is that, as of March 27th, Bitdeer's short-selling positions accounted for 39.83% of its circulating shares. This means that for every 5 shares of circulating stock in the market, nearly 2 shares were lent out for short selling, with short sellers closely watching the uncertainties arising from their aggressive expansion. At this point, all the suspense and pressure finally boiled down to a single piece of paper—the contract for the AI ​​business. During the earnings call in February 2026, a core question repeatedly asked by analysts was: had a lease been signed for the 180 MW project in Norway? The CFO's response at the time was that they were in intensive negotiations with well-known companies in the industry. Their super blueprints have been laid out, and interest bills are being generated daily on schedule. Now, let's see who moves into that 180 megawatts.
